---
title: Mandatory Assignment 1
author: Astrid Haarup Møller, Esmeralda Larsson and Nanna Munch Kjøller
date: 2026-06-03
date-format: MMMM DD, YYYY
execute: 
  echo: false
  warning: false
format:
  pdf: 
    number-sections: true
    colorlinks: true
    geometry:
      - top=20mm
      - left=20mm
      - bottom=20mm
      - right=20mm
    bibliography: references.bib
    cite-method: natbib
    biblio-style: apalike  
    lang: en
    fontsize: 12pt
    highlight-style: github-light
---


## Quarto - a framework for reproducible research

Quarto enables you to weave together content and executable code into a finished document. To learn more about Quarto see <https://quarto.org>. When you click the **Preview** button in Positron or type `quarto render` in the terminal a .pdf document will be generated that includes both content and the output of embedded code. The template below can be used to submit your answers for the mandatory assignment. Within the template you find all typical issues. By default, code is omitted in the output (can be changed with `echo: true` in line 6). Formatting is done using markdown (e.g., check out how to use \# to define sections and subsections or how to generate bold text). Footnotes and citations are easy. You can also include math notation using LaTeX style. The template contains inline code execution (e.g. to automatically return the ticker of the asset with the highest Sharpe ratio) and shows how to embed figures into the document.

## Exercise

1. We download data for the S\&P 500 index (`^GSPC`) and the 13-week Treasury Bill rate (`^IRX`) from Yahoo! Finance for the period January 1, 2000 to February 28, 2026. Following the assignment instructions, the `^IRX` series is interpreted as an annualized yield in percent and converted into a monthly risk-free rate as
$$
r_{f,t} = (1 + IRX_t/100)^{1/12} - 1.
$$
The monthly excess market return is then computed as
$$
\tilde r_{m,t} = r_{m,t} - r_{f,t},
$$
where $r_{m,t}$ denotes the monthly return on the S\&P 500 index. :contentReference[oaicite:1]{index=1}


```{python}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import tidyfinance as tf
from plotnine import *
from mizani.formatters import percent_format
```

```{python} 
#| echo: true
#| cache: true

ticker = ["AAPL", "MSFT", "BA"]
prices = tf.download_data(
  domain="stock_prices", 
  symbols=ticker,
  start_date="2000-01-01", 
  end_date="2025-12-31"
)

returns = (prices
  .assign(ret=lambda x: x.groupby("symbol")["adjusted_close"].pct_change())
  .get(["symbol", "date", "ret"])
  .dropna(subset="ret")
)
```

1. Visualize the distribution of the daily returns.

```{python}
#| label: fig-return-distribution
#| fig-cap: Distribution of daily stock returns. 

returns_figure = (
  ggplot(returns, aes(x="ret", fill="symbol")) +
  geom_histogram(bins=100) +
  labs(x="", y="",
       fill ="") +
  facet_wrap("symbol") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels=percent_format()) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(legend_position = "none")
)
returns_figure.draw()
```

@fig-return-distribution shows the distribution of daily returns for the three assets.

1. Compute the sample mean $\mu$ and the variance-covariance matrix $\Sigma$ of the returns.
Which of the individual assets delivered the highest Sharpe ratio (assume the risk-free rate is zero) during the sample period?^[Whenever reporting performance measures, compute annualized values and clearly state how you calculated the measure.]
The estimated moments may later be used for the construction of optimal portfolios (@Markowitz1952; @Merton1972)

```{python}
returns_matrix = (prices
  .pivot(columns="symbol", 
         values="adjusted_close", 
         index="date")
  .pct_change()
  .dropna()
)

mu = np.array(returns_matrix.mean()).T
sigma = np.array(returns_matrix.cov())

sharpe_ratio = pd.DataFrame({"ticker": ticker,
  "Sharpe": np.sqrt(250) * mu / np.sqrt(np.diag(sigma))})
```

**Answer: ** The asset with the highest annualized Sharpe-ratio, computed as 
$$
\sqrt{250}\frac{\hat\mu_i}{\hat\sigma_i},
$$

where $\hat\mu_i$ is the sample mean of asset $i$s dailies returns and $\hat\sigma_i$ is the standard deviation of asset $i$s dailys returns is `{python} sharpe_ratio.loc[sharpe_ratio['Sharpe'] == sharpe_ratio['Sharpe'].max(), 'ticker'].iloc[0]` with an annualized Sharpe-ratio of `{python} round(sharpe_ratio['Sharpe'].max(), 3)`. @tbl-sharpe-ratios illustrates all Sharpe ratios. 

```{python}
#| tbl-cap: Historical annualized Sharpe-ratios
#| label: tbl-sharpe-ratios
#| output: asis
from great_tables import GT
tbl = (
    GT(sharpe_ratio)
    .fmt_number(columns="Sharpe", decimals=3)
)
latex_tbl = tbl.as_latex()
print(latex_tbl)
```

## References
::::{#refs}
::::
